subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

A librarian is organizing a collection of books that are arranged in an arithmetic sequence by their publication year. The first book was published in year 2,6,10,14,…2,6,10,14,\dots.

  1. Calculate, showing relevant stages in the working, the common difference, dd.

  2. Determine, showing relevant stages in the working, the 1010th term in the sequence.

  3. Find, showing relevant stages in the working, the sum of the first 1010 terms in the sequence.
markscheme_body:
1. We have u1=2u_1 = 2 and u2=6u_2 = 6. Hence the common difference is

    d=u2−u1=6−2=4\begin{aligned} d &= u_2 - u_1 \hspace{1.5em} \\[6pt] &= 6 - 2 \\[6pt] &\answer{= 4}\end{aligned}

  2. Using the nnth term formula un=u1+(n−1)du_n = u_1 + (n-1)d with n=10n = 10, we get

    u10=u1+(10−1)d=2+(10−1)(4)=38\begin{aligned} \hspace{0.8em} u_{10} &= u_1 + (10-1)d \\[6pt] &= 2 + (10-1)(4) \\[6pt] &\answer{= 38} \end{aligned}

  3. Using the sum of nn terms formula Sn=n2(u1+un)S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}(u_1 + u_n) with n=10n=10, we obtain

    S10=102(u1+u10)=102(2+38)=200\begin{aligned} S_{10} &= \dfrac{10}{2}(u_1 + u_{10}) \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{10}{2}(2 + 38) \\[11pt] &\answer{= 200} \end{aligned}
question_html:
Question 1

Calculator Not Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

Consider an arithmetic sequence 2,6,10,14,…2,6,10,14,\dots

  1. Find the common difference, dd.

  2. Find the 1010th term in the sequence.

  3. Find the sum of the first 1010 terms in the sequence.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

A music streaming service reports that a particular artist had exactly 120,000 streams on Day 1, 145,000 streams on Day 2, and 170,000 streams on Day 3. The number of daily streams follows an arithmetic sequence.

  1. Calculate the common difference, dd, showing relevant stages in the working.


  2. Find the number of streams predicted for Day 5, u8u_8, showing relevant stages in the working.


  3. Determine the total number of streams at the end of the first 7 days, S8S_8, showing relevant stages in the working.
markscheme_body:
1. The common difference is

    d=u2−u1=32−40=−8\begin{aligned} d &= u_2 - u_1 \hspace{1.5em} \\[6pt] &= 32 - 40 \\[6pt] &\answer{= -8}\end{aligned}

  2. Using the nnth term formula un=u1+(n−1)du_n = u_1 + (n-1)d with n=8n = 8, we get

    u8=u1+(8−1)d=40+(8−1)(−8)=−16\begin{aligned} \hspace{2em} u_8 &= u_1 + (8-1)d \\[6pt] &= 40 + (8-1)(-8) \\[6pt] &\answer{= -16}\end{aligned}

  3. Using the sum of nn terms formula Sn=n2(u1+un)S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}(u_1 + u_n) with n=8n=8, we obtain

    S8=82(u1+u8)=82(40+(−16))=96\begin{aligned} \hspace{1em} S_8 &= \dfrac{8}{2}(u_1 + u_8) \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{8}{2}(40 + (-16)) \\[11pt] &\answer{= 96}\end{aligned}
question_html:
Question 2

Calculator Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

An arithmetic sequence has u1=40u_1= 40, u2=32u_2 = 32, u3=24u_3 = 24.

  1. Find the common difference, dd.

  2. Find u8u_8.

  3. Find S8S_8.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

Only one of the following four sequences is arithmetic and only one of them is geometric.

an=1, 5, 10, 15, …  cn=1.5, 3, 4.5, 6, …bn=12, 23, 34, 45, …  dn=2, 1, 12, 14, …\begin{array}{rcccccl} a_n &=& 1,\,5,\,10,\,15,\,\dots &\,\hspace{4em}\,& c_n &=& 1.5,\,3,\,4.5,\,6,\,\dots \\[12pt] b_n &=& \dfrac{1}{2},\,\dfrac{2}{3},\,\dfrac{3}{4},\,\dfrac{4}{5},\,\dots &\,\hspace{4em}\,& d_n &=& 2,\,1,\,\dfrac{1}{2},\,\dfrac{1}{4},\,\dots \end{array}

  1. State which sequence is arithmetic and find the common difference of the sequence.

  2. State which sequence is geometric and find the common ratio of the sequence.

  3. For the geometric sequence, find the exact value of the eighth term. Give your answer as a fraction.
markscheme_body:
1. We have

    a2−a1≠a3−a2    c2−c1=c3−c25−1≠10−53−1.5=4.5−34≠51.5=1.5b2−b1≠b3−b2d2−d1≠d3−d223−12≠34−231−2≠12−116≠112−1≠−12 \begin{array}{rclc|crcl} a_2-a_1 &\neq& a_3-a_2 &\,\hspace{0.5em}\,&\,\hspace{0.5em}\,& c_2-c_1 &=& c_3-c_2 \\[12pt] 5-1 &\neq& 10-5 &&& 3-1.5 &=& 4.5-3 \\[12pt] 4 &\neq& 5 &&& 1.5 &=& 1.5 \\[12pt] \hline &&&&&&& \\ b_2-b_1 &\neq& b_3-b_2 &&& d_2-d_1 &\neq& d_3-d_2 \\[12pt] \dfrac{2}{3}-\dfrac{1}{2} &\neq& \dfrac{3}{4}-\dfrac{2}{3} &&& 1-2 &\neq& \dfrac{1}{2}-1 \\[12pt] \dfrac{1}{6} &\neq& \dfrac{1}{12} &&& -1 &\neq& -\dfrac{1}{2} \end{array} \vspace{0.95em}

    Hence cn\answer{c_n} is arithmetic and d=1.5\answer{d = 1.5}

  2. We have

    a2a1≠a3a2    c2c1≠c3c251≠10531.5≠4.535≠22≠1.5b2b1≠b3b2d2d1=d3d22/31/2≠3/42/312=1/2143≠9812=12 \begin{array}{rclc|crcl} \dfrac{a_2}{a_1} &\neq& \dfrac{a_3}{a_2} &\,\hspace{0.5em}\,&\,\hspace{0.5em}\,& \dfrac{c_2}{c_1} &\neq& \dfrac{c_3}{c_2} \\[12pt] \dfrac{5}{1} &\neq& \dfrac{10}{5} &&& \dfrac{3}{1.5} &\neq& \dfrac{4.5}{3} \\[12pt] 5 &\neq& 2 &&& 2 &\neq& 1.5 \\[12pt] \hline &&&&&&& \\ \dfrac{b_2}{b_1} &\neq& \dfrac{b_3}{b_2} &&& \dfrac{d_2}{d_1} &=& \dfrac{d_3}{d_2} \\[12pt] \dfrac{2/3}{1/2} &\neq& \dfrac{3/4}{2/3} &&& \dfrac{1}{2} &=& \dfrac{1/2}{1} \\[12pt] \dfrac{4}{3} &\neq& \dfrac{9}{8} &&& \dfrac{1}{2} &=& \dfrac{1}{2} \end{array} \vspace{0.95em}

    Hence dn\answer{d_n} is geometric and r=12\answer{r = \dfrac{1}{2}}

  3. Using the nnth term formula dn=d1rn−1d_n = d_1r^{n-1} with n=8n = 8, we get

    d8=d1r8−1=2(12)8−1=164\begin{aligned} d_8 &= d_1r^{8-1} \\[8pt] &= 2\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{8-1} \\[8pt] &\answer{= \dfrac{1}{64}}\end{aligned}
question_html:
Question 3

Calculator Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

Only one of the following four sequences is arithmetic and only one of them is geometric.

an=1, 5, 10, 15, …  cn=1.5, 3, 4.5, 6, …bn=12, 23, 34, 45, …  dn=2, 1, 12, 14, …\begin{array}{rcccccl} a_n &=& 1,\,5,\,10,\,15,\,\dots &\,\hspace{4em}\,& c_n &=& 1.5,\,3,\,4.5,\,6,\,\dots \\[12pt] b_n &=& \dfrac{1}{2},\,\dfrac{2}{3},\,\dfrac{3}{4},\,\dfrac{4}{5},\,\dots &\,\hspace{4em}\,& d_n &=& 2,\,1,\,\dfrac{1}{2},\,\dfrac{1}{4},\,\dots \end{array}

  1. State which sequence is arithmetic and find the common difference of the sequence.

  2. State which sequence is geometric and find the common ratio of the sequence.

  3. For the geometric sequence find the exact value of the eighth term. Give your answer as a fraction.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

Only one of the following four sequences is arithmetic and only one of them is geometric.

an=13, 14, 15, 16, …  cn=3, 1, 13, 19, …bn=2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, …  dn=1, 3, 6, 10, …\begin{array}{rcccccl} a_n &=& \dfrac{1}{3},\,\dfrac{1}{4},\,\dfrac{1}{5},\,\dfrac{1}{6},\,\dots &\,\hspace{4em}\,& c_n &=& 3,\,1,\,\dfrac{1}{3},\,\dfrac{1}{9},\,\dots \\[12pt] b_n &=& 2.5,\,5,\,7.5,\,10,\,\dots &\,\hspace{4em}\,& d_n &=& 1,\,3,\,6,\,10,\,\dots \end{array}

  1. State which sequence is arithmetic and find the common difference of the sequence.


  2. State which sequence is geometric and find the common ratio of the sequence.


  3. For the geometric sequence, find the exact value of the sixth term. Give your answer as a fraction.
markscheme_body:
1. We have

    a2−a1≠a3−a2    c2−c1≠c3−c214−13≠15−141−3≠13−1−112≠−120−2≠−23b2−b1=b3−b2d2−d1≠d3−d25−2.5=7.5−53−1≠6−32.5=2.52≠3 \begin{array}{rclc|crcl} a_2-a_1 &\neq& a_3-a_2 &\,\hspace{0.5em}\,&\,\hspace{0.5em}\,& c_2-c_1 &\neq& c_3-c_2 \\[12pt] \dfrac{1}{4}-\dfrac{1}{3} &\neq& \dfrac{1}{5}-\dfrac{1}{4} &&& 1-3 &\neq& \dfrac{1}{3}-1 \\[12pt] -\dfrac{1}{12} &\neq& -\dfrac{1}{20} &&& -2 &\neq& -\dfrac{2}{3} \\[12pt] \hline &&&&&&& \\ b_2-b_1 &=& b_3-b_2 &&& d_2-d_1 &\neq& d_3-d_2 \\[12pt] 5-2.5 &=& 7.5-5 &&& 3-1 &\neq& 6-3 \\[12pt] 2.5 &=& 2.5 &&& 2 &\neq& 3 \end{array} \vspace{1.2em}

    Hence bn\answer{b_n} is arithmetic and d=2.5\answer{d = 2.5}

  2. We have

    a2a1≠a3a2    c2c1=c3c21/41/3≠1/51/413=1/3134≠4513=13b2b1≠b3b2d2d1≠d3d252.5≠7.5531≠632≠1.53≠2 \begin{array}{rclc|crcl} \dfrac{a_2}{a_1} &\neq& \dfrac{a_3}{a_2} &\,\hspace{0.5em}\,&\,\hspace{0.5em}\,& \dfrac{c_2}{c_1} &=& \dfrac{c_3}{c_2} \\[12pt] \dfrac{1/4}{1/3} &\neq& \dfrac{1/5}{1/4} &&& \dfrac{1}{3} &=& \dfrac{1/3}{1} \\[12pt] \dfrac{3}{4} &\neq& \dfrac{4}{5} &&& \dfrac{1}{3} &=&\dfrac{1}{3} \\[12pt] \hline &&&&&&& \\ \dfrac{b_2}{b_1} &\neq& \dfrac{b_3}{b_2} &&& \dfrac{d_2}{d_1} &\neq& \dfrac{d_3}{d_2} \\[12pt] \dfrac{5}{2.5} &\neq& \dfrac{7.5}{5} &&& \dfrac{3}{1} &\neq& \dfrac{6}{3} \\[12pt] 2 &\neq& 1.5 &&& 3 &\neq& 2 \end{array} \vspace{0.95em}

    Hence cn\answer{c_n} is geometric and r=13\answer{r = \dfrac{1}{3}}

  3. Using the nnth term formula cn=c1rn−1c_n = c_1r^{n-1} with n=6n = 6, we get

    c6=c1r6−1=3(13)6−1=181\begin{aligned} c_6 &= c_1r^{6-1} \\[8pt] &= 3\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^{6-1} \\[8pt] &\answer{= \dfrac{1}{81}}\end{aligned}
question_html:
Question 4

Calculator Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

Only one of the following four sequences is arithmetic and only one of them is geometric.

an=13, 14, 15, 16, …  cn=3, 1, 13, 19, …bn=2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, …  dn=1, 3, 6, 10, …\begin{array}{rcccccl} a_n &=& \dfrac{1}{3},\,\dfrac{1}{4},\,\dfrac{1}{5},\,\dfrac{1}{6},\,\dots &\,\hspace{4em}\,& c_n &=& 3,\,1,\,\dfrac{1}{3},\,\dfrac{1}{9},\,\dots \\[12pt] b_n &=& 2.5,\,5,\,7.5,\,10,\,\dots &\,\hspace{4em}\,& d_n &=& 1,\,3,\,6,\,10,\,\dots \end{array}

  1. State which sequence is arithmetic and find the common difference of the sequence.

  2. State which sequence is geometric and find the common ratio of the sequence.

  3. For the geometric sequence find the exact value of the sixth term. Give your answer as a fraction.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

A music streaming service reports that a particular indie band gained 450 new listeners in the first week after releasing their album. The number of new listeners each week follows an infinite geometric sequence, with 135 new listeners in the second week, 40.5 in the third week, and so on.

  1. Calculate the common ratio, rr, showing relevant stages in the working.


  2. Find the 2020th term of the sequence, representing the number of new listeners in that week, showing relevant stages in the working.


  3. Determine the exact sum of the infinite sequence, which represents the total number of new listeners the band will ever gain from this album, showing relevant stages in the working.
markscheme_body:
1. We have u1=4480u_1 = 4480 and u2=−3360u_2 = -3360. Hence the common ratio is

    r=u2u1=−33604480=−0.75\begin{aligned} r &= \dfrac{u_2}{u_1} \\[6pt] &= -\dfrac{3360}{4480} \\[12pt] &\answer{= -0.75}\end{aligned}

  2. Using the nnth term formula un=u1rn−1u_n = u_1 r^{n-1} with n=20n = 20, we get

    u20=u1r20−1=(4480)(−0.75)20−1≈−18.9\begin{aligned} \hspace{4em} u_{20} &= u_1 r^{20-1} \\[6pt] &= (4480)(-0.75)^{20-1} \\[7pt] &\answer{\approx -18.9}\end{aligned}

  3. Using the sum of an infinite geometric sequence formula S∞=u11−rS_{\infty} = \dfrac{u_1}{1-r}, we find

    S∞=44801−(−0.75)=2560\begin{aligned} \hspace{1.3em} S_{\infty} &= \dfrac{4480}{1-(-0.75)} \\[8pt] &\answer{= 2560}\end{aligned}
question_html:
Question 5

Calculator Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

Consider the infinite geometric sequence 44804480, −3360-3360, 25202520, −1890,…-1890,\dots

  1. Find the common ratio, rr.

  2. Find the 2020th term.

  3. Find the exact sum of the infinite sequence.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

A music streaming service tracks the number of listens for three different songs over the first four weeks after release. The data is shown in the table below for sequences unu_n, vnv_n, and wnw_n.

  1. State which sequence is

    1. arithmetic;

    2. geometric.


  2. Find the sum of the first 5050 terms of the arithmetic sequence, showing all relevant working.


  3. Calculate the exact value of the 1313th term of the geometric sequence, showing all relevant working.
markscheme_body:
1. vn\answer{v_n}

    2. wn\answer{w_n}

  2. The common difference of the arithmetic sequence is

    d=v2−v1=24−12=12\begin{aligned} d &= v_2 - v_1 \hspace{0.75em} \\[6pt] &= 24 - 12 \\[6pt] &= 12\end{aligned}

    Hence, using the sum of nn terms formula Sn=n2(2v1+(n−1)d)S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}(2v_1 + (n-1)d) with n=50n=50,

    we get

    S50=502(2v1+(50−1)d)=502(2(12)+(50−1)(12))=15300\begin{aligned} \hspace{6em} S_{50} &= \dfrac{50}{2}(2v_1 + (50-1)d) \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{50}{2}(2(12) + (50-1)(12)) \\[11pt] &\answer{= 15\hspace{0.15em}300}\end{aligned}

  3. The common ratio of the geometric sequence is

    r=w2w1=2412=2\begin{aligned} r &= \dfrac{w_2}{w_1} \hspace{2.4em} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{24}{12} \\[12pt] &= 2\end{aligned}

    Hence, using the nnth term formula wn=w1rn−1w_n = w_1r^{n-1} with n=13n = 13, we get

    w13=w1r13−1=12(2)13−1=49152\begin{aligned} \hspace{3.1em} w_{13} &= w_1r^{13-1} \\[6pt] &= 12\hspace{0.05em}(2)^{13-1} \hspace{3.8em} \\[7pt] &\answer{= 49\hspace{0.15em}152}\end{aligned}
question_html:
Question 6

Calculator Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

The table shows the first four terms of three sequences: unu_n, vnv_n, and wnw_n.

  1. State which sequence is

    1. arithmetic;

    2. geometric.

  2. Find the sum of the first 5050 terms of the arithmetic sequence.

  3. Find the exact value of the 1313th term of the geometric sequence.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 7]

A music streaming playlist features songs with durations forming an arithmetic sequence: 33 seconds, 55 seconds, 7,…7,\dots seconds.

  1. Obtain the value of the common difference, dd, by extracting information, with little or no calculation.


  2. Obtain an answer showing relevant stages in the working.

    1. u10u_{10};

    2. S10S_{10}.


  3. Given that un=253u_n = 253, find the value of nn.
markscheme_body:
1. We have u1=3u_1 = 3 and u2=5u_2 = 5. Hence the value of dd is

    d=u2−u1=5−3=2\begin{aligned} d &= u_2 - u_1 \\[6pt] &= 5 - 3 \\[6pt] &\answer{= 2}\end{aligned}

    1. Using the nnth term formula un=u1+(n−1)du_n = u_1 + (n-1)d with n=10n = 10, we get

      u10=u1+(10−1)d=3+(10−1)(2)=21\begin{aligned} \hspace{0.1em} u_{10} &= u_1 + (10-1)d \\[6pt] &= 3 + (10-1)(2) \\[6pt] &\answer{= 21}\end{aligned}

    2. Using the sum of nn terms formula Sn=n2(u1+un)S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}(u_1 + u_n) with n=10n=10, we find

      S10=102(u1+u10)=102(3+21)=120\begin{aligned} S_{10} &= \dfrac{10}{2}(u_1 + u_{10}) \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{10}{2}(3 + 21) \\[11pt] &\answer{= 120}\end{aligned}

  3. Substituting un=253u_n = 253, u1=3u_1 = 3 and d=2d = 2 in un=u1+(n−1)du_n = u_1 + (n-1)d and solving the resulting equation for nn, we obtain

    253=3+(n−1)(2)253=2n+1252=2n\begin{aligned} \hspace{2.6em} 253 &= 3 + (n-1)(2) \\[6pt] 253 &= 2n+1 \\[6pt] 252 &= 2n\end{aligned} n=126\answer{n = 126}\hspace{1.1em}
question_html:
Question 7

Calculator Not Allowed

[Maximum mark: 7]

An arithmetic sequence is given by 33, 55, 7,…7,\dots

  1. Write down the value of the common difference, dd.

  2. Find

    1. u10u_{10};

    2. S10S_{10}.

  3. Given that un=253u_n = 253, find the value of nn.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

A digital music platform sees that the number of streams for a newly released song follows an infinite geometric sequence. The first four weekly stream counts are 90009000, −7200-7200, 57605760, −4608-4608, ...

  1. Calculate the common ratio, showing relevant stages in the working.


  2. Find the 2525th term of the sequence, showing relevant stages in the working.


  3. Determine the exact sum of the infinite sequence, showing relevant stages in the working.
markscheme_body:
1. We have u1=9000u_1 = 9000 and u2=−7200u_2 = -7200. Hence the common ratio is

    r=u2u1=−72009000=−45\begin{aligned} r &= \dfrac{u_2}{u_1} \\[8pt] &= -\dfrac{7200}{9000} \hspace{2.9em} \\[8pt] &\answer{= -\dfrac{4}{5}} \end{aligned}

  2. Using the nnth term formula un=u1rn−1u_n = u_1r^{n-1} with n=25n = 25, we find

    u25=u1r25−1=9000(−45)25−1≈42.5\begin{aligned} u_{25} &= u_1r^{25-1} \\[6pt] &= 9000\left(\hspace{-0.2em}-\dfrac{4}{5}\right)^{25-1} \\[8pt] &\answer{\approx 42.5}\end{aligned}

  3. Using the sum of an infinite geometric sequence formula S∞=u11−rS_{\infty} = \dfrac{u_1}{1-r}, we get

    S∞=90001−(−4/5)=5000\begin{aligned} S_{\infty} &= \dfrac{9000}{1-(-4/5)} \hspace{1.9em} \\[9pt] &\answer{= 5000}\end{aligned}
question_html:
Question 8

Calculator Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

Consider the infinite geometric sequence 90009000, −7200-7200, 57605760, −4608-4608, ...

  1. Find the common ratio.

  2. Find the 2525th term.

  3. Find the exact sum of the infinite sequence.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

Maya deposits $8000\$8000 into a high-yield digital wallet that offers an annual interest rate of 5.55.5%, compounded annually.

  1. nn years. Obtain the answer(s) by extracting information, with little or no calculation. a formula which calculates the total value of the investment after


  2. Obtain a numerical answer showing the relevant stages in the working. the amount of money in the digital wallet after:

    1. 11 year;

    2. 33 years.


  3. Maya plans to use the funds to make a $10000\$10\hspace{0.15em}000 down payment on a solar-powered electric scooter. Obtain the only possible answer. if Maya will be able to afford this within a 55-year timeframe.
markscheme_body:
1. We have

    Hence, using the compound interest formula, we have

    FV=PV(1+r100k)kn=8000(1+5.5100×1)1×n=8000(1+0.055)n(1)\begin{aligned} \mathrm{FV} &= \mathrm{PV}\bigg(1 + \dfrac{r}{100k}\bigg)^{kn} \\[5pt] &= 8000\bigg(1 + \dfrac{5.5}{100\times1}\bigg)^{1\times n} \\[12pt] &\answer{= 8000\hspace{0.05em}(1+0.055)^n} \hspace{3em}{(1)}\end{aligned}

    1. Substituting n=1n = 1 in the formula (1)(1), we get

      FV=8000(1+0.055)=$8440\begin{aligned} \mathrm{FV} &= 8000\hspace{0.05em}(1+0.055) \hspace{0.5em} \\[8pt] &\answer{= \$8440}\end{aligned}

    2. Substituting n=3n = 3 in the formula (1)(1), we get

      FV=8000(1+0.055)3≈$9390\begin{aligned} \mathrm{FV} &= 8000\hspace{0.05em}(1+0.055)^3 \\[8pt] &\answer{\approx \$9390}\end{aligned}

  3. Substituting n=5n = 5 in the formula (1)(1), we obtain

    FV=8000(1+0.055)5≈$10456\begin{aligned} \hspace{2.2em} \mathrm{FV} &= 8000\hspace{0.05em}(1+0.055)^5 \\[8pt] &\approx \$10\hspace{0.15em}456\end{aligned}

    Yes, Jeremy will be able to do this within a 5-year timeframe.\answer{\text{Yes, Jeremy will be able to do this within a \(5\)-year timeframe.}}
question_html:
Question 9

Calculator Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

Jeremy invests $8000\$8000 into a savings account that pays an annual interest rate of 5.55.5 %, compounded annually.

  1. Write down a formula which calculates that total value of the investment after nn years.

  2. Calculate the amount of money in the savings account after:

    1. 11 year;

    2. 33 years.

  3. Jeremy wants to use the money to put down a $10000\$10\hspace{0.15em}000 deposit on an apartment. Determine if Jeremy will be able to do this within a 55-year timeframe.

subject: Mathematics AA HL Practice V2
question_body:
[Maximum mark: 6]

Consider the following sequence of patterns formed by matchsticks used to create squares.

Pattern 1 contains 66 line segments.

  1. Given that Pattern nn contains 101101 line segments, show that n=20n = 20.


  2. Show all relevant stages of working. Find the total number of line segments in the first 2020 patterns.
markscheme_body:
1. We have an arithmetic sequence with u1=6u_1 = 6, u2=11u_2 = 11 and u3=16u_3 = 16.

    The common difference is

    d=u2−u1=11−6=5\begin{aligned} d &= u_2 - u_1 \\[6pt] &= 11 - 6 \\[6pt] &= 5\end{aligned}

    Hence, if we substitute un=101u_n = 101, u1=6u_1 = 6 and d=5d = 5 in un=u1+(n−1)du_n = u_1 + (n-1)d and solve the resulting equation for nn, we get

    101=6+(n−1)(5)101=5n+1100=5n\begin{aligned} \hspace{1.9em} 101 &= 6 + (n-1)(5) \\[6pt] 101 &= 5n+1 \\[6pt] 100 &= 5n\end{aligned} n=20\answer{n = 20}\hspace{2.3em}

  2. Using the sum of nn terms formula Sn=n2(u1+un)S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}(u_1 + u_n) with n=20n=20, we obtain

    S20=202(u1+u20)=202(6+101)=10(107)=1070\begin{aligned} \hspace{1.5em} S_{20} &= \dfrac{20}{2}(u_1 + u_{20}) \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{20}{2}(6 + 101) \\[12pt] &= 10(107) \\[16pt] &\answer{= 1070}\end{aligned}
question_html:
Question 10

Calculator Not Allowed

[Maximum mark: 6]

Consider the following sequence of figures.

Figure 1 contains 66 line segments.

  1. Given that Figure nn contains 101101 line segments, show that n=20n = 20.

  2. Find the total number of line segments in the first 2020 figures.
